findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 7

findings.label: Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
findings.evidence: Deck has 19 slides (minimum 8)

findings.label: Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?
findings.evidence: Revenue/currency figures found

findings.label: Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've identified a real market problem and sized it well, but investors will want to know you've actually talked to the people who need to solve it—add a slide showing 3-5 specific customer conversations (even brief ones) that validate the pain points you're describing, like "spoke with 4 villa developers in Dubai who cited maintenance costs and reliability as top blockers to solar adoption" or "pilot with [developer name] showed our design reduced overheating failures by 60% vs. legacy systems." This doesn't need to be a full case study yet, just proof that real customers recognize the problem you're solving.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they feel like a checklist—add one concrete timeline or enforcement milestone that creates urgency right now. For example, instead of just "Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)," say something like "Dubai's 2024 Estidama update now requires solar hot water on 80% of new villas by 2025, creating immediate compliance pressure for 200+ active projects." This shows investors that developers aren't choosing to adopt your solution—they're being forced to solve this problem in the next 12 months, which is why now is the moment to capture market share.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature wishlist — you need to show investors what actually exists versus what's still vapor. Add one concrete example to Slide 19 showing how the AI engine works in practice: for instance, "Given a 200m² villa in Dubai with 4 occupants, HelioHelp outputs: 12-collector system, AED45K installed cost, 18-month ROI, 8 tonnes CO2 savings annually" — this proves the core algorithm is real and working, not just planned. Without this, investors will assume you're still in the ideation phase when you're actually much further along.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a brief section to your Technology slide listing the key third-party dependencies that power HelioHelp — for example, "Built on [cloud provider], integrates [mapping/weather API], uses [LLM framework]" — so investors understand what external systems you rely on and that you've thought through vendor lock-in or API deprecation risks. This also gives you a chance to highlight any competitive advantages (like proprietary training data) that reduce your exposure to those dependencies.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You're operating in regulated markets (UAE, Abu Dhabi, GCC) with mandated solar installations and utility partnerships, but your deck doesn't mention data security, system certifications, or compliance frameworks that buyers will require before signing. Add a single bullet on Slide 5 (Technology) stating something like "ISO 50001 Energy Management certified + GDPR-compliant HelioHelp platform" or whatever certifications you actually hold—this removes a friction point investors will ask about anyway, especially when they see utility and government clients in your pipeline.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides present financial projections with revenue figures and scenarios (Bear/Base/Bull) but do not explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'GTM via developers/ESCOs' but do not provide the underlying mathematical assumptions or detailed cost breakdowns that would constitute stated assumptions.
findings.suggestion: Add a single assumptions box to your financials slide that shows the three drivers of your Base case: units sold per year (starting with 50 in 2026, growing to X by 2030), average selling price per installation, and opex as a % of revenue. For example: "2026: 50 units × $6K ASP = $300K revenue; 2027: 150 units × $7K ASP = $1.05M" — this lets investors reverse-engineer your numbers and builds confidence that your projections aren't arbitrary.

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.evidence: $350K seed funding: 18-month cash runway

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You've got gross margins and one payback period (Rixos at 3.1 years), but investors need to see the full unit economics story: what's your actual CAC for hotels versus villas, what's the LTV based on those gross margins and contract length, and what's your LTV:CAC ratio telling you about unit profitability? Add a simple table showing CAC, contract value, gross profit per unit, and payback period broken out by segment (hotels vs. villas vs. licensing partners) — this will prove your model scales efficiently and isn't just relying on one pilot's success.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy between the $213K contracted amount and the $300K projected revenue.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 14, your 2026 hardware revenue shows $0.3M, but Slide 12 only lists $213K in contracted deals—that's a $87K gap that investors will immediately spot and question your forecasting credibility. Clarify whether the $300K includes projected pipeline conversions beyond the two named customers (like the Masdar City or Abu Dhabi Ports discussions), and if so, explicitly call out which opportunities you're modeling into that $300K figure with realistic close probabilities.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing exactly how you'll deploy the new capital you're raising—break it down by category (e.g., "Manufacturing tooling: $X," "Sales & ops hiring: $X," "Working capital for Q2-Q3 deployments: $X") and tie each line item to a specific milestone or revenue driver from your pipeline. Right now investors see your $213K contracted revenue and $350K seed runway, but they don't know if you're raising $500K, $2M, or $5M, or what that money actually does to accelerate the Rixos, EtihadWE, and Masdar deals you've already lined up.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
findings.evidence: EBITDA positive 2027

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to the Rixos and EtihadWE bullets stating the payment status—something like "Rixos (live project, revenue received Q3 2024)" or "EtihadWE (contract signed, $X paid to date)"—so investors see these aren't just pilots but actual paying customers generating real cash. Right now it reads like they're still in deployment mode, which kills momentum.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got the revenue streams and unit economics down, but investors need to see momentum—add actual growth numbers like "Revenue grew from $X in Month 1 to $Y in Month 3" or "Rixos project generated $Z in Q1, up X% from pilot phase." This proves your model is working, not just theoretically sound.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got a live customer in Rixos that's been running your system for over a year—that's gold, so show it. Add a single metric to the Rixos case study: "System uptime: 99.2%" or "Zero unplanned maintenance in 12 months" or whatever your actual data shows, then call it out as proof of your reliability claim on the "Why SolarisKit Wins" slide. Investors need to see that your systems don't just work in theory—they stay working, which is what keeps customers from churning to competitors.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project), EtihadWE

findings.label: Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.evidence: The slide presents a competitive landscape table comparing 'Legacy Solar', 'Heat Pumps', 'Electric Storage' as generic categories, not specific named competitors
findings.suggestion: Replace those generic categories with actual competitor names your GCC customers would recognize — something like "Daikin Heat Pumps," "Tesla Powerwall," and "traditional flat-plate solar from local installers" — so investors immediately understand which real products you're beating and why your positioning matters in that specific market.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why SolarisKit beats existing solutions, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Carrier, or a well-funded solar company decides to build exactly what you're building. Add a section called "Why Now, Why Us" that explains one structural barrier to entry—for example: "Heat pump manufacturers lack the flat-roof solar expertise (our 3 years of Gulf climate testing), while solar companies lack thermal engineering talent and refrigerant certification requirements that take 18+ months to obtain." This shows investors you've thought about defensibility, not just current competition.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing a chance to leverage outside validation—add a small "Advisors" section with 2-3 people who bring credibility in areas your team doesn't fully cover (like venture scaling, enterprise sales channels, or clean tech policy). For example, if you have someone like a former VP of Sales at a scaled cleantech company or a board member from a major solar manufacturer who's advising you, that signals to investors that experienced operators believe in your approach and can open doors your team might not have yet.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your team slide stating the vesting schedule for key founders—something like "Dr. Faisal Ghani: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" next to his bio. Investors need to see that founder equity is locked in to reduce flight risk, and it's a quick credibility signal that you've thought through retention incentives for the team that's executing your post-raise plan.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification mentioned on Slide 3, which is great, but investors in regulated markets need to see the full compliance picture upfront—add a dedicated compliance slide before the financials that maps out which UAE building codes (Estidama, Dubai Municipality, Barjeel) your system meets, what certifications you already hold versus what's in progress, and the timeline to full regulatory sign-off for scaled deployments. For example: "Solar Keymark ✓ | Dubai Municipality Approval (Q2 2026) | Estidama Compliance (Q3 2026) | RAK Building Code Certification (In Progress)" so investors see you're not just

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong IP protection with your US, EU, and UK patents, but investors will want explicit confirmation that there are no pending disputes, liens, or encumbrances against the company or your technology—add a single bullet to your IP slide (Slide 8) stating something like "No pending litigation, IP disputes, or encumbrances" to close that question proactively and build confidence in your clean legal standing. This is especially important given you're in due diligence with Masdar and seeking institutional capital—a simple, direct statement removes friction.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these align. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing how the 5% UAE market share ($20M) connects to your $400M SAM — for example: "5% UAE market share of $400M SAM = $20M annual revenue opportunity" or show the calculation explicitly so investors see the math is internally consistent, not just a standalone claim. Right now it reads as disconnected, which makes the whole market sizing feel less credible even though the underlying logic is sound.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, and Slide 3 labeled '[UNKNOWN]' suggests incomplete deck structure
findings.suggestion: Go through the deck systematically and replace every instance of [URL_REDACTED] and [UNKNOWN] with actual content or remove the placeholder entirely—Slide 3's title should read "Solution" or "Product" (not [UNKNOWN]), and every [URL_REDACTED] needs either the real URL, a source citation, or deletion if it's not essential. Also fix the cut-off text on Slide 6 where "Strong push for dec" should complete to "decarbonisation" to match the context below it. These gaps make the deck look unfinished to investors, so a final proofread pass catching these three issues will instantly raise the polish level.
